explicit_text: I was born and raised in Japan, specifically in Okayama-ken, Taka-machi. When I decided to come to America in the early 1920s, it was a difficult choice because many people in Japan discouraged us from leaving. Life in Japan during the Taishō period was quite different from what I found in America, and I often felt homesick after arriving. Despite the challenges, I maintained strong Japanese instincts and tried to raise my children with an understanding of our culture.

explicit_text: When I first arrived in America, I landed in San Francisco before going on to other places like Stockton and Marysville. San Francisco was the main port where many Japanese immigrants disembarked, and from there, we all dispersed to different communities. It was a starting point for many of us, but soon after arrival, we separated to join our husbands or find work elsewhere.

explicit_text: While working on the railroad in the mountains near Portland, the cook was from Kumamoto Prefecture. Sometimes he would speak in the Kumamoto dialect, which was hard for us to understand. It was interesting to hear different Japanese dialects even while living so far from Japan.

explicit_text: We left Japan from the port of Yokohama on July 4th, the day of America's Independence Day, which was marked by fireworks. Yokohama was the last place I saw before embarking on the long journey to America. It was a bittersweet moment because I was leaving my homeland but also starting a new life.
